title: Call Out Sick Politely
summary: Notify your workplace that you cannot come in due to illness in a brief, respectful way.
learnerPromise: You will be able to inform your workplace that you are too sick to work using the method they prefer, with the right information and a respectful tone.
whyItMatters: Calling out sick properly protects your coworkers from illness, helps your workplace arrange coverage, and shows you are responsible even when you cannot work.
realLifeUses: Calling a manager the morning of a shift because you have a fever and cannot work safely
Sending a text through a workplace app to report that you are vomiting and need to stay home
Notifying your workplace about a contagious illness like the flu or a stomach bug
Reporting an unexpected injury that makes it impossible to do your job safely
materialsNeeded: Your supervisor's or workplace's contact number or messaging method
Your employee ID or identifying information if required
A general idea of when you might return
result: A completed sick notification sent through the correct channel with all required information.
youWillLearn: How to find the correct way to report an absence at your workplace
What basic information to include: your name, shift time, and that you are unwell
How to keep the message brief and honest without oversharing medical details
How to follow your workplace's policy on sick days and doctor's notes
miniChallenge: Write or practice a 30-second sick-call script for an imaginary workplace. Include your name, that you are unwell, and when you expect to return.
steps: Check your employee handbook or posted policy for the correct way to call out sick — phone call, text, app, or email.
Contact your supervisor or the designated person as early as possible, ideally at least one to two hours before your shift starts.
State clearly: your name, the fact that you are sick and cannot work, and the date and time of your shift.
Give a general sense of when you expect to return, such as 'I hope to be back for my next scheduled shift' if that seems realistic.
Thank them for understanding and end the call or message. Do not go into long medical details unless specifically asked.
completionCriteria: You notified the correct person using the method your workplace requires
Your message included your name, the shift you are missing, and a brief statement that you are unwell
commonProblems: Not knowing the correct contact method or number — find this out before you ever need it
Waiting too long to call — notify as early as you can so your workplace can find coverage
Feeling guilty and offering too many medical details — a simple 'I am unwell and cannot work' is enough
Not following up if you are out for multiple days — check in daily if your workplace expects it
tips: Save your supervisor's number and the call-out line in your phone before you need them
Know your workplace's policy on doctor's notes — some require them after a certain number of days
If you have a contagious illness, mentioning that you are protecting coworkers can show responsibility
Keep a record of when you called and who you spoke with, in case of payroll questions later
